title: Ancient Persia
description: Practice your skills with a quiz on ancient Persia

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage and answer the question.

>"When I entered Babylon as a friend and when I established the seat of the government in the palace of the ruler under jubilation and rejoicing, Marduk, the great lord, induced the forgiving inhabitants of Babylon to love me, and I was daily trying to worship him . . . I returned to these sacred cities on the other side of the Tigris River the sanctuaries which have been ruins for a long time, the images which used to live in them and established for them permanent sanctuaries. I also gathered all their former inhabitants and returned them to their original homes."

>From Cyrus the Great’s Decree of Return for the Jews, 539 BCE

**The events described in this passage can be best understood in which of the following contexts?**


[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The captivity of Jews in the Babylonian Empire
The conquest of Jerusalem by the Romans
The abolition of religious freedom in Persia
The Assyrian conquest of Israel

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage and answer the question. 

>"There is no nation which so readily adopts foreign customs as the Persians. Thus, they have taken the dress of the Medes, considering it superior to their own; and in war they wear the Egyptian breastplate. As soon as they hear of any luxury, they instantly make it their own."

From: Herodotus, _Histories_440 BCE.

**If Herodotus’ description of the Persians was correct, it would have contributed most directly to which of the following?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The ability of Persian rulers to impose political unity on diverse populations
The need to force cultural similarity on all subjects under Persian rule
Ongoing conflicts between Persia and Greece due to cultural differences
The use of foreign soldiers in the military of the Persian Empire

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 2]]

**The Behistun Inscription above best illustrates which historical continuity between empires?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Image of the Behistun Inscription in modern day Iran.
options.caption: The Behistun Inscription was commissioned by Persian ruler Darius I and listed the king's important deeds in three different languages. The Inscription was placed along a major roadway. Image credit: [Wikimedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Behistun_Inscription#/media/File:Darius_I_the_Great%27s_inscription.jpg)

choices.content: They tried to politically unify diverse populations
They tried to religiously unify diverse populations
They tried to maintain the loyalty of elites
They tried to create and enforce social hierarchies

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

**The construction of the Royal Road shown in the map above during the height of the Persian Empire best illustrates which of the following similarities between classical period empires?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Map showing the Achaemenid Persian Empire at its greatest extent. The Royal Road is shown in the broken black line running from Susa to Sardis.
options.caption: Map showing the Achaemenid Persian Empire at its greatest extent. The Royal Road is shown in the broken black line running from Susa to Sardis. Image credit: [Wikimedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Achaemenid_Empire#/media/File:The_Achaemenid_Empire_at_its_Greatest_Extent.jpg)

choices.content: The centralization of government often led to a need for more transportation and communication networks
The need to protect border regions often led to the construction of military defenses on the edges of empire
Expanded transportation and communication networks developed independently of centralized states
Because empires built larger, more centralized administrative systems, there was less need for transportation systems

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following best explains the context for building the road shown in this map?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Map showing the Achaemenid Persian Empire at its greatest extent. The Royal Road is shown in the broken black line running from Susa to Sardis.
options.caption: Map showing the Achaemenid Persian Empire at its greatest extent. The Royal Road is shown in the broken black line running from Susa to Sardis. Image credit: [Wikimedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Achaemenid_Empire#/media/File:The_Achaemenid_Empire_at_its_Greatest_Extent.jpg)

choices.content: The road addressed concerns about both external and internal enemies by allowing the projection of military and political power
The road allowed rapid deployment of military forces to frontier regions to defend against potential invasion by nomadic peoples
The road reflected a desire to prevent foreign trade, so the main road was built to stay entirely within within Persian territory
The road was part of a military expansion that used transportation networks to expand Persian control over foreign territories

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following best explains the decision to place this inscription along a major roadway?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Image of the Behistun Inscription in modern day Iran.
options.caption: The Behistun Inscription was commissioned by Persian ruler Darius I and listed the king's important deeds in three different languages. The Inscription was placed along a major roadway. Image credit: [Wikimedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Behistun_Inscription#/media/File:Darius_I_the_Great%27s_inscription.jpg)

choices.content: Heavy use of transportation and communication networks provided a way for rulers to reach large audiences with their messages
The importance of divine kingship in Achaemenid Persia led Darius I to commission and build this inscription to show his religious devotion
The inscription provided a way of communicating Persian legal codes to new subjects and foreign travelers by being placed in a prominent spot
The inscription was written in three languages in order to encourage cultural divisions among subject peoples and prevent political unification

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 1]]

**Which of the following was a result of the introduction of standardized currencies like the one shown above?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Gold Daric, one of the standard coins of the Achaemenid Empire introduced by Darius the Great.
options.caption: Gold Daric, one of the standard coins of the Achaemenid Empire introduced by Darius the Great. Image credit: [Wikimedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Achaemenid_Empire#/media/File:Double_daric_330-300_obverse_CdM_Paris.jpg)

choices.content: Increased trade due to standardized currency making transactions easier
Decreased tax revenue due to people not using new standardized currency
Decreased need for administrative institutions as the central state got smaller
Increased use of legal systems to regulate trade and production

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 3]]

[[☃ image 2]]

**The royal palace complex in this photo and drawing could best be used as evidence for which of the following claims about Persepolis’ role in the Persian Empire?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Plans of the site of Persepolis, the capital city built by Darius the Great.
options.caption: Plans of the site of Persepolis, the capital city built by Darius the Great. Image credit: [Wikimedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Persepolis#/media/File:Plan_of_Persepolis.png).

options.alt: Photo of the ruins of Persepolis.
options.caption: Photo of the ruins of Persepolis. Image credit: [Wikimedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Persepolis#/media/File:Persepolis_12.jpg)

choices.content: It served primarily as an administrative center
It served primarily as a religious center
It served primarily as a trade center
It served primarily as a military center

assessment_items.question_content:
[[☃ image 3]]

[[☃ image 1]]

**The construction of Persepolis, the ruins of which are shown above, in a mountainous region is best understood in the context of which of the following?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

options.alt: Plans of the site of Persepolis, the capital city built by Darius the Great.
options.caption: Plans of the site of Persepolis, the capital city built by Darius the Great. Image credit: [Wikimedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Persepolis#/media/File:Plan_of_Persepolis.png)

options.alt: Photo of the ruins of Persepolis.
options.caption: Photo of the ruins of Persepolis. Image credit: [Wikimedia](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Persepolis#/media/File:Persepolis_12.jpg)

choices.content: The growing power and wealth of the Persian Empire allowed for the construction of a new administrative center
Alexander the Great’s invasion caused the Persians to build a new capital city that was hidden in mountains
The site and layout were chosen because of their significance to the Zoroastrian belief system
The site was the original homeland of the Persians and the city was built there before the Persian Empire expanded

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage and answer the question. 

>"Judging by the Aramaic inscriptions discovered in Persepolis, there were also workshops in Arachosia, where vessels were manufactured for the court of the Persian rulers. It appears that these workshops included free craftsmen, who were paid for their labor or who were fulfilling state labor service for a certain period of time . . . there were relatively few slaves (when compared with free individuals), while free people remained the basic workers both in agriculture and in the crafts." 

From Muhammad Dandamaev and Vladimir Lukonin, _The Culture and Social Institutions of Ancient Iran_ (1989).

**This passage is best understood in which of the following Achaemenid Persian contexts?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The maintenance of social hierarchies based on labor
The abolition of slavery due to concerns about individual rights
The lack of standardized currency to pay and collect taxes
The production of goods specifically for foreign trade

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage and answer the question. 

>"The real weakening of Achaemenid power . . . came from the independent actions of the satraps [governors] of Anatolia [modern-day Turkey]. About 368-367 BCE the satrap of Cappadocia, Datames, openly proclaimed his independence from central authority . . . The time-honored practice of the [Achaemenid royal] court, setting one satrap against another . . . in the end failed, and Ariobarzanes, satrap in Daskyleion, joined Datames in revolt." 

>From Richard N. Frye, _The History of Ancient Iran_ (1983). 

**The events described in the passage best illustrate which of the following developments?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The system for maintaining the loyalty of elites to the central government began to break down
Discontent among peasant farmers led to uprisings and rebellions
Increased centralization of government stabilized regional unrest
The threat of foreign invasion prevented satraps from independently revolting

assessment_items.question_content:
Read the passage and answer the question. 

>"During the Achaemenid period significant changes occurred in the system of land relations in the Middle East. All the land that was under cultivation was accurately measured, and the best portion of it belonged to the king, the temples, business houses, military leaders, and the civil servants of the royal house and temple administration."  

From Muhammad Dandamaev and Vladimir Lukonin, _The Culture and Social Institutions of Ancient Iran_ (1989).

**This passage best illustrates which of the following similarities between classical empires?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: The creation of systems to manage production in order to reward elites for loyalty
The expansion of legal systems in order to control trade
The growth of imperial cities due to increased use of religious rituals
The erosion of political institutions due to concentration of wealth
